page: Vultr vc2-1c-2gb is an entry-level virtual machine within the Cloud Compute family. It is configured with a single shared vCPU based on the x86_64 architecture, utilizing an Intel Xeon processor running at 2.0 GHz. The system includes 2.0 GB of RAM, providing 2.0 GB of memory per core, and is equipped with 55 GB of local NVMe SSD storage. Additionally, one complimentary public IPv4 address is allocated to the instance.

Performance benchmarks indicate that this server operates in the lower tiers of processing capability. Both single-core and multi-core stress-ng CPU tests recorded a score of 1211.89, placing the instance in the bottom 10% to 25% of tested hardware. Memory bandwidth is also constrained, with read-only large block speeds at 7.23 GB/sec and write large block speeds at 5.37 GB/sec. The Passmark CPU score of 1141.0 and database operations score of 285.19 reflect the limited throughput of the shared Intel Xeon core.

The primary tradeoff of this instance is its shared CPU allocation, which can lead to variable performance depending on host node activity. However, the inclusion of local NVMe SSD storage provides a baseline of fast storage access relative to the low-resource profile. This server is positioned as a budget-friendly option for entry-level workloads that do not require sustained high-performance compute. Appropriate workloads include basic static web serving, lightweight development environments, simple scripting tasks, and small-scale testing.

The hardware configuration includes 2.0 GB of RAM and a 55 GB NVMe SSD, along with one complimentary public IPv4 address. Benchmark data indicates constrained performance, with a Passmark CPU score of 1141.0 and a stress-ng CPU score of 1211.89, placing it in the lower performance tiers. Memory bandwidth is limited, showing 7.23 GB/sec for large block reads. Due to its shared CPU allocation and low resource density, this instance represents a budget-friendly option with clear performance tradeoffs. It is best suited for low-demand workloads such as static web serving, basic scripting, lightweight testing, and small development environments.
